choices: B) To store and manage bean definitions before instantiation
C) To inject dependencies into beans
D) To handle bean lifecycle callbacks
gradedQuestions.prompt: What is the primary role of BeanDefinitionRegistry in Spring?
What does a ComponentScanner do when it encounters a class annotated with @Component?

choices: A) They are the same thing
B) Registration stores metadata (BeanDefinition), instantiation creates the actual object
C) Registration creates objects, instantiation stores metadata
D) Registration happens at runtime, instantiation at compile time
gradedQuestions.prompt: What is the key difference between bean registration and bean instantiation?
In our exercise, ComponentScanner.scan() takes an explicit Class[] list instead of scanning a package. What is the practical reason for this simplification?

expectedPoints: The container needs to know all beans before creating any, so it can resolve dependencies
Separation enables lazy initialization, scope management, and circular dependency detection
feedbackHints: Think about what happens when Bean A depends on Bean B which depends on Bean C
reflectionQuestions.prompt: Why does Spring separate bean registration (storing BeanDefinitions) from bean creation (instantiation)?
rubric: Mentions separation of metadata from actual objects
Mentions benefits like lazy init, scope control, or dependency ordering
